---
title: "HW_Power_Analysis"
author: "Elizabeth, Ken, Manav, Nancy"
date: "2026-06-13"
output: pdf_document
bibliography: power_analysis.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(data.table)
library(lmtest)
library(sandwich)
library(ggplot2)
library(stargazer)
```

# Introduction

Our experiment utilizes a digital, logic-based puzzle game to evaluate how external framing influences user task persistence and performance. The game consists of **five** discrete rounds, with each round featuring a distinct logic puzzle drawn from three core categories: numerical sequences, alphabetical sequences, and visual pattern sequences. To eliminate order bias, the presentation sequence of the rounds will be fully randomized for each participant.

To optimize our statistical power given recruitment constraints, we intentionally reduced our scope from a full $3 \times 3$ factorial design to a streamlined $1 \times 3$ single-factor design (No Label vs. Accurate Label vs. Under-stated Label). Rather than fragmenting our participant pool across multiple puzzle difficulties and label variations, we isolate our treatments exclusively within a highly complex tier. By anchoring the task within this maximum 'frustration window,' we optimize our signal-to-noise ratio and ensure the simulation captures the most robust behavioral signal driven by cognitive dissonance.

When a participant registers to play, they are randomly assigned to one of three experimental groups. This assignment dictates how the difficulty of the puzzles is framed throughout the session:

- **Control Group (No Label)**: Puzzles are presented with no difficulty indicators.

- **Treatment Group 1 (Accurate Label)**: Puzzles are prefaced with a "Hard" indicator, which accurately reflects the high objective complexity of the selected sequences.

- **Treatment Group 2 (Understated Label)**: Puzzles are systematically prefaced with an "Easy" or "Beginner" indicator, serving as a psychological understatement of the true difficulty.

Our primary outcome variable is **Total Time Spent per Round (seconds)**, capped at 600 seconds. We want to capture two distinct behavioral tracks:

- **The Time-to-Solve Track**: This tracks cognitive velocity. It measures how much longer it takes participants to complete rounds.

- **The Time-to-Quit Track**: This measures cognitive exhaustion or frustration.

As a secondary exploratory analysis on our real data, we also track **Frustration Clicks** to map the behavioral intensity of the cognitive dissonance. Using micro-level definitions outlined by [@ayolov2025rage], we define a frustration click as an interaction event when a participant triggers three or more rapid clicks on the exact same UI element (such as the "Submit" button) within a narrow, short interval of 1.5 seconds.

# Justification of Simulation Parameters

To anchor our power analysis simulation in realistic boundaries, we model our parameters after an ego-depletion laboratory experiments conducted by [@baumeister1998ego]. In Experiment 1 of their study, the researchers evaluated 56.4% drop in volitional endurance by measuring how long hungry participants would persist at a challenging spatial puzzle task before quitting. Depleted control participants experiments a 56.4% drop in performance duration due to prior cognitive friction. In our power analysis simulation, we use the following parameters:

1.  **Control Baseline Mean**: Because we expect our logic rounds to be completed in roughly 4-5 minutes, we establish a baseline mean of 270 seconds ($\mu_{control} = 270$).

2.  **Population Standard Deviation**: We set the standard deviation to 90 seconds ($\sigma = 90$). We chose this because it is conservative where individual user variance spans roughly $\pm 1.5$ minutes around our 270-second average.

# Global Functions for Scenario Simulation

```{r}
#| echo: true
set.seed(123)

# Defining baseline global parameters (control group)
mean_solve_time <- 270  # Average seconds expected per round
sd_solve_time   <- 100  # Conservative individual time spread

# Data-Generating Function
generate_scenario_data <- function(N, n_rounds,
                                   mean_solve_time, sd_solve_time, easy_solve_effect, 
                                   hard_solve_effect,
                                   p_quit_5_control, p_quit_5_easy, p_quit_5_hard,
                                   mean_rage_control, mean_rage_hard, mean_rage_easy) {
                                     
  # Initialize data table for N participants
  d <- data.table(id = 1:N)
  
  # Random assignment (3-way equal splits)
  group_size <- N / 3
  d[ , treatment := sample(c(rep('no_label', group_size),
                             rep('hard_label', group_size),
                             rep('easy_label', group_size)))]
  
  # Multi-round structural loop (Rounds 1 to 5)
  for (i in 1:n_rounds) {
    # Dynamically construct unique column names for each round
    solve_time_col <- paste0("r_", i, "_time_to_solve")
    quit_time_col  <- paste0("r_", i, "_time_to_quit")
    total_time_col <- paste0("r_", i, "_total_time_spent")
    rage_click     <- paste0("r_", i, "_rage_clicks")
  
    # Calculate solve times based on group effects
    current_mu_solve <- fcase(
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'hard_label', mean_solve_time + hard_solve_effect,
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'easy_label', mean_solve_time + easy_solve_effect,
      default = mean_solve_time
    )
    
    # Map the group-specific quit probabilities dynamically at the 5-minute mark
    current_mu_quit <- fcase(
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'no_label',   p_quit_5_control,
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'hard_label', p_quit_5_hard,
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'easy_label', p_quit_5_easy,
      default = 0
    )
    
    # Clamp to valid probability range just in case
    current_mu_quit <- pmin(pmax(as.numeric(current_mu_quit), 0), 1)
    
    # Map distinct rage click expectations
    current_lambda <- fcase(
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'no_label',   mean_rage_control,
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'hard_label', mean_rage_hard,
      as.character(d$treatment) == 'easy_label', mean_rage_easy,
      default = 0
    )
    
    # Generate underlying timelines with natural variance
    d[ , (solve_time_col) := pmax(10, current_mu_solve + rnorm(n = .N, mean = 0, sd = sd_solve_time))]
    d[ , (quit_time_col)  := as.numeric(NA)]
    
    # At 5-minutes, users are shown a button to skip to the next round or continue 
    # working on the current round.
    
    # Users who are still stuck at 5 minutes (300 seconds)
    stuck_at_5_min <- d[[solve_time_col]] > 300    
    # Who decides to skip after 5 minutes based on group probability
    quits_at_5_min <- stuck_at_5_min & (rbinom(n = nrow(d), size = 1, 
                                               prob = as.numeric(current_mu_quit)) == 1)
    # Record actual quit times after the prompt appears
    d[quits_at_5_min, (quit_time_col) := 300 + runif(.N, 1, 5)]
    
    # At 10 minutes, this is the round hard stop
    forced_timeout <- (d[[solve_time_col]] > 600) & (!quits_at_5_min)
    d[forced_timeout, (quit_time_col) := 600]
    
    is_final_quit <- !is.na(d[[quit_time_col]])
    
    # If they quit/timeout, solve time becomes NA. If they completed it, quit time stays NA
    d[is_final_quit,  (solve_time_col) := as.numeric(NA)]
    d[!is_final_quit, (quit_time_col)  := as.numeric(NA)]
    
    # Generate cumulative engagement time
    d[ , (total_time_col) := pmax(get(solve_time_col), get(quit_time_col), na.rm = TRUE)]
    
    # Generate click count metrics
    d[ , (rage_click) := rpois(n = .N, lambda = current_lambda)]
  }
  setkey(d, id)
  return(d)
}
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
# Reshapes simulated experimental data from "Wide Format" (where multiple rounds sit 
# side-by-side in columns) to "Long Format" (where rounds are stacked vertically as 
# separate rows) so that we can run regressions with interaction effects without 
# violating row independence.

convert_to_long_format <- function(wide_dataset) {
  # Dynamically capture how many rounds exist based on your new column layout
  solve_cols <- grep("_time_to_solve$", names(wide_dataset), value = TRUE)
  num_rounds <- length(solve_cols)
  
  # Melt using your exact new variable patterns: r_i_...
  long_df <- melt(
    wide_dataset,
    id.vars = c("id", "treatment"),
    measure.vars = list(
      paste0("r_", 1:num_rounds, "_time_to_solve"),
      paste0("r_", 1:num_rounds, "_time_to_quit"),
      paste0("r_", 1:num_rounds, "_total_time_spent"),
      paste0("r_", 1:num_rounds, "_rage_clicks")
    ),
    value.name = c("time_to_solve", "time_to_quit", "total_time_spent", "rage_clicks")
  )
  
  # Standardize the index tracking column into an explicit round counter
  setnames(long_df, "variable", "round_num")
  long_df[, round_num := as.numeric(round_num)]
  
  return(long_df)
}
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
run_power_analysis <- function(long_dataset, outcome_var = "total_time_spent",
                                target_coef = "treatmenteasy_label",
                                power_loops = 1000) {
  
  # The inner sampling-and-regression function
  one_test <- function(dataset, sample_size) {
    # Isolate unique participant IDs to respect cluster structure
    unique_ids <- unique(dataset[["id"]])
    n_unique_total <- length(unique_ids)
    
    # Scale unique participant targets by the sample size fraction
    n_participants_target <- round(n_unique_total * sample_size)
    
    # Sample complete participant histories (all 5 rounds stay together)
    sampled_ids <- sample(unique_ids, n_participants_target)
    
    # Use which() to safely pull complete matching rows along with all column attributes
    matching_rows <- which(dataset$id %in% sampled_ids)
    d_sample <- dataset[matching_rows, ]
    
    # Establish 'no_label' as our baseline reference level
    d_sample$treatment <- factor(d_sample$treatment,
                                  levels = c('no_label', 'hard_label', 'easy_label'))
    
    # Run panel regression with interaction effects using dynamic outcome variable
    formula <- as.formula(paste0(outcome_var, " ~ treatment * round_num"))
    model <- lm(formula, data = d_sample)
    
    # Compute standard errors clustered by participant 'id'
    clustered_output <- coeftest(model, vcov = vcovCL(model, cluster = ~id))
    
    # Extract the corrected, robust p-value for your target coefficient
    # Safely return NA if coefficient doesn't exist in this sample
    if (target_coef %in% rownames(clustered_output)) {
      clustered_output[target_coef, 'Pr(>|t|)']
    } else {
      NA
    }
  }

  samples_list <- c(.1, .2, .3, .4, .5, .6, .7, .8, .9, 1.0)
  power_results <- list()
  
  # 1,000 Simulation Replicator Loop across fractional steps
  for (sample_size in samples_list) {
    p_values <- replicate(power_loops, one_test(long_dataset, sample_size))
    
    # Track the percentage of parallel universes where we successfully reject the null
    power_results[[as.character(sample_size)]] <- mean(p_values <= 0.05, na.rm = TRUE)
  }
  
  # Compute the true human baseline participant count for accurate X-Axis charting
  total_participants <- length(unique(long_dataset[["id"]]))
  
  # Return the clean, simplified plotting frame
  data.frame(
    sample_size = as.numeric(names(power_results)) * total_participants,
    power       = unlist(power_results)
  )
}
```

# Scenario 1 (Accurate labeling and mislabeling leads to increased solve time and reduced probability of quitting)

In this scenario, we simulated how understating task difficulty causes players to take longer due to an ego threat. When a hard task is misleadingly labeled "easy," it triggers immediate self-doubt when a player struggles. According to [@marshall2004expectations], this ego threat creates a state of divided concentration; instead of focusing entirely on the puzzle, the player's attention is fractured by anxiety. This emotional distraction may impact cognitive efficiency and lengthen the time it takes to complete the task.

We also model a distinct behavioral shift between the baseline ("No Label") and congruent ("Hard Label") conditions regarding completion times. While both groups are tackling the identical difficult puzzle, players exposed to the "Hard" label are hypothesized to take longer to solve it. According to Expectation Confirmation Theory [@oliver1980cognitive], upfront framing calibrates a user’s cognitive strategy. When there is a "Hard" label up front, this correct expectation allows the player to slow down, adopt a deliberate, methodical, and systematic problem-solving strategy, and mentally budget more time for the task. We also modeled a lower chance of quitting compared to the control.

We also shifted our quit probabilities after the 5-minute prompt. In this scenario, we hypothesize that 50% of the control group will quit due to attention decay. Additionally, we think puzzles congruently labeled as "Hard" will have a lower chance of quitting because a difficult framing may activate a challenge mindset and encourage players to persist through anticipated obstacles (@lucas2015when).

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: hide
# Generating data for Scenario 1
scenario_1_d <- generate_scenario_data(
  N = 300,        # Total sample size across all 3 groups (100 per group)
  n_rounds = 5,   # Simulating 5 rounds of gameplay
  
 # Solve Time Baseline & Shifting Effects
  mean_solve_time   = 270,   # Baseline control group mean (no label)
  sd_solve_time     = 90,    # Conservative individual spread
  hard_solve_effect = 25,    # Accurate label takes longer than baseline (+25s)
  easy_solve_effect = 35,    # Understated label takes slightly longer (+35s)
  
  # 5-Minute Prompt Quit Probabilities (Longer time to quit = lower probability)
  p_quit_5_control = 0.50,   # Baseline: 50% of stuck control users quit at 5 mins
  p_quit_5_hard    = 0.30,   # Accurate: 30% quit (higher quit prob = shorter quit time)
  p_quit_5_easy    = 0.15,   # Understated: Only 15% quit (lowest prob = longest patience/quit time)
  
  # Frustration Metrics (Poisson expected average events)
  mean_rage_control = 0.4,   # Low baseline frustration
  mean_rage_hard    = 0.8,   # Moderate friction
  mean_rage_easy    = 0.9    # Highest frustration (broken expectations)
)


# Reshape the data so that it's stacked vertically
scenario_1_long <- convert_to_long_format(scenario_1_d)
scenario_1_long[, treatment := relevel(factor(treatment), ref = "no_label")]

# Run regressions for Scenario 1
s1 <- lm(total_time_spent ~ treatment * round_num, data = scenario_1_long)
frustration_s1 <- lm(rage_clicks ~ treatment * round_num, data = scenario_1_long)

s1_clustered_se <- sqrt(diag(vcovCL(s1, cluster = ~id, data = scenario_1_long)))
frustration_clustered_se <- sqrt(diag(vcovCL(frustration_s1, cluster = ~id, data = scenario_1_long)))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| warning: false
#| message: false
stargazer(
  s1, frustration_s1,
  type = "latex",
  title = "Scenario 1: Panel Regression with Clustered Standard Errors",
  style = "default",
  
  # Overrides standard OLS SEs with clustered SEs
  se = list(s1_clustered_se, frustration_clustered_se),
  
  covariate.labels = c(
    "Easy / Understated Label",
    "Hard / Accurate Label",
    "Round Number (Time Trend)",
    "Interaction: Easy Label × Round",
    "Interaction: Hard Label × Round"
  ),
  dep.var.labels = c("Total Seconds Spent", "Rage Clicks"),
  notes = c(
    "Baseline Intercept represents the Control Group (No Label).",
    "Standard errors are clustered at the unique participant (id) level."
  ),
  notes.append = FALSE,
  digits = 2
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
easy_time_coef <- round(coef(s1)["treatmenteasy_label"], 1)
hard_time_coef <- round(coef(s1)["treatmenthard_label"], 1)
easy_rage_coef <- round(coef(frustration_s1)["treatmenteasy_label"], 1)
hard_rage_coef <- round(coef(frustration_s1)["treatmenthard_label"], 1)
easy_time_p    <- round(coeftest(s1, vcov = vcovCL(s1, cluster = ~id))["treatmenteasy_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
hard_time_p    <- round(coeftest(s1, vcov = vcovCL(s1, cluster = ~id))["treatmenthard_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
easy_rage_p    <- round(coeftest(frustration_s1, vcov = vcovCL(frustration_s1, cluster = ~id))["treatmenteasy_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
hard_rage_p    <- round(coeftest(frustration_s1, vcov = vcovCL(frustration_s1, cluster = ~id))["treatmenthard_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
```

The regression successfully detected both treatment effects in the simulated data. The Easy Label group spent significantly more time per round than control (+`r easy_time_coef`s, p \< .001), and the Hard Label group did as well (+`r hard_time_coef`s, p \< .001). The frustration signal was also cleanly detected; the Easy Label group triggered +`r easy_rage_coef` more rage clicks per round than control (p \< .001), and the Hard Label group +`r hard_rage_coef` (p \< .001). Interaction terms were non-significant, indicating both effects are stable across rounds.

```{r running the power analysis}
s1_power_time <- run_power_analysis(
  long_dataset = scenario_1_long,
  outcome_var  = "total_time_spent",
  target_coef  = "treatmenteasy_label",
  power_loops  = 1000
)

print(s1_power_time)

s1_power_rage <- run_power_analysis(
  long_dataset = scenario_1_long,
  outcome_var  = "rage_clicks",
  target_coef  = "treatmenteasy_label",
  power_loops  = 1000
)

print(s1_power_rage)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
power_80_time <- s1_power_time$sample_size[which(s1_power_time$power >= 0.80)[1]]
power_95_time <- s1_power_time$sample_size[which(s1_power_time$power >= 0.95)[1]]
power_at_200  <- round(s1_power_time$power[s1_power_time$sample_size == 210], 2) * 100
power_80_rage <- s1_power_rage$sample_size[which(s1_power_rage$power >= 0.80)[1]]
power_at_200_rage <- round(s1_power_rage$power[s1_power_rage$sample_size == 210], 2) * 100
```

Our simulation-based power analysis sampled fractions of our 300-participant synthetic dataset 1,000 times per step, fitting both panel regressions with participant-clustered standard errors at each increment. For our primary outcome, power didn't cross the 80% conventional threshold until `r power_80_time` total participants (`r round(power_80_time/3)` per group) — which is what anchors our recruitment target at **300 participants (\~100 per group)**. Our secondary frustration outcome tells a different story; the rage click model hits 80% power at just `r power_80_rage` participants (`r round(power_80_rage/3)` per group), meaning the frustration signal is strong enough to detect with far less data. We size the study to the harder-to-detect outcome to make sure we're adequately powered across both.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.show: show
s1_power_time$outcome  <- "Total Time Spent"
s1_power_rage$outcome  <- "Rage Clicks"
power_combined <- rbind(s1_power_time, s1_power_rage)

# Plot
ggplot(power_combined, aes(x = sample_size, y = power, color = outcome)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = 0.82, label = "80% Power Threshold", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = "gray40") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(30, 300, by = 30)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Scenario 1 Power Analysis by Sample Size",
    x        = "Number of Participants",
    y        = "Statistical Power",
    color    = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Scenario 2

This scenario is an alternate version of scenario 1, with increased treatment effects on time to solve. The goal is to see how many participants we need for a sufficiently powered test with a higher treatment effect.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: hide
# Generating data for Scenario 2
scenario_2_d <- generate_scenario_data(
  N = 300,        # Total sample size across all 3 groups (100 per group)
  n_rounds = 5,   # Simulating 5 rounds of gameplay
  
 # Solve Time Baseline & Shifting Effects
  mean_solve_time   = 270,   # Baseline control group mean (no label)
  sd_solve_time     = 90,    # Conservative individual spread
  hard_solve_effect = 35,    # Accurate label takes moderately longer than baseline (+35s)
  easy_solve_effect = 55,    # Understated label takes much longer (+55s)
  
  # 5-Minute Prompt Quit Probabilities (Longer time to quit = lower probability)
  p_quit_5_control = 0.50,   # Baseline: 50% of stuck control users quit at 5 mins
  p_quit_5_hard    = 0.30,   # Accurate: 30% quit (higher quit prob = shorter quit time)
  p_quit_5_easy    = 0.15,   # Understated: Only 15% quit (lowest prob = longest patience/quit time)
  
  # Frustration Metrics (Poisson expected average events)
  mean_rage_control = 0.4,   # Low baseline frustration
  mean_rage_hard    = 0.8,   # Moderate friction
  mean_rage_easy    = 0.9    # Highest frustration (broken expectations)
)

# Reshape the data so that it's stacked vertically
scenario_2_long <- convert_to_long_format(scenario_2_d)
scenario_2_long[, treatment := relevel(factor(treatment), ref = "no_label")]

# Run regressions for Scenario 2
s2 <- lm(total_time_spent ~ treatment * round_num, data = scenario_2_long)
frustration_s2 <- lm(rage_clicks ~ treatment * round_num, data = scenario_2_long)

s2_clustered_se <- sqrt(diag(vcovCL(s2, cluster = ~id, data = scenario_2_long)))
frustration_clustered_se <- sqrt(diag(vcovCL(frustration_s2, cluster = ~id, data = scenario_2_long)))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| warning: false
#| message: false
stargazer(
  s2, frustration_s2,
  type = "latex",
  title = "Scenario 2: Panel Regression with Clustered Standard Errors",
  style = "default",
  
  # Overrides standard OLS SEs with clustered SEs
  se = list(s2_clustered_se, frustration_clustered_se),
  
  covariate.labels = c(
    "Easy / Understated Label",
    "Hard / Accurate Label",
    "Round Number (Time Trend)",
    "Interaction: Easy Label × Round",
    "Interaction: Hard Label × Round"
  ),
  dep.var.labels = c("Total Seconds Spent", "Rage Clicks"),
  notes = c(
    "Baseline Intercept represents the Control Group (No Label).",
    "Standard errors are clustered at the unique participant (id) level."
  ),
  notes.append = FALSE,
  digits = 2
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
easy_time_coef <- round(coef(s2)["treatmenteasy_label"], 1)
hard_time_coef <- round(coef(s2)["treatmenthard_label"], 1)
easy_rage_coef <- round(coef(frustration_s2)["treatmenteasy_label"], 1)
hard_rage_coef <- round(coef(frustration_s2)["treatmenthard_label"], 1)
easy_time_p    <- round(coeftest(s2, vcov = vcovCL(s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenteasy_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
hard_time_p    <- round(coeftest(s2, vcov = vcovCL(s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenthard_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
easy_rage_p    <- round(coeftest(frustration_s2, vcov = vcovCL(frustration_s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenteasy_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
hard_rage_p    <- round(coeftest(frustration_s2, vcov = vcovCL(frustration_s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenthard_label", "Pr(>|t|)"], 3)
easy_interaction_p<-coeftest(s2, vcov = vcovCL(s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenteasy_label:round_num", "Pr(>|t|)"]
hard_interaction_p<-coeftest(s2, vcov = vcovCL(s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenthard_label:round_num", "Pr(>|t|)"]
easy_interaction_coef <- round(coef(s2)["treatmenteasy_label:round_num"], 1)
hard_interaction_coef <- round(coef(s2)["treatmenthard_label:round_num"], 1)

rage_easy_interaction_p<-coeftest(s2, vcov = vcovCL(frustration_s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenteasy_label:round_num", "Pr(>|t|)"]
rage_hard_interaction_p<-coeftest(s2, vcov = vcovCL(frustration_s2, cluster = ~id))["treatmenthard_label:round_num", "Pr(>|t|)"]
rage_easy_interaction_coef <- round(coef(frustration_s2)["treatmenteasy_label:round_num"], 1)
rage_hard_interaction_coef <- round(coef(frustration_s2)["treatmenthard_label:round_num"], 1)

```

Participants exposed to either difficulty label spent significantly more time on the task and exhibited greater frustration than those in the no‑label control condition. The easy/understated label produced the largest effects, increasing total time spent by `r easy_time_coef` seconds and raising rage‑click behavior by r`easy_rage_coef` clicks on average. The hard/accurate label also yielded significant increases `r hard_time_coef` additional seconds spent and `r hard_rage_coef` more rage clicks relative to the control group. No other predictors reached statistical significance, indicating that the primary behavioral impacts arise directly from the presence and type of difficulty label rather than from time trends or changes across rounds.

```{r running the power analysis for scenario 2}
s2_power_time <- run_power_analysis(
  long_dataset = scenario_2_long,
  outcome_var  = "total_time_spent",
  target_coef  = "treatmenteasy_label",
  power_loops  = 1000
)

print(s2_power_time)

s2_power_rage <- run_power_analysis(
  long_dataset = scenario_2_long,
  outcome_var  = "rage_clicks",
  target_coef  = "treatmenteasy_label",
  power_loops  = 1000
)

print(s2_power_rage)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
power_80_time <- s2_power_time$sample_size[which(s2_power_time$power >= 0.80)[1]]
power_95_time <- s2_power_time$sample_size[which(s2_power_time$power >= 0.95)[1]]
power_at_200  <- round(s2_power_time$power[s2_power_time$sample_size == 210], 2) * 100
power_80_rage <- s2_power_rage$sample_size[which(s2_power_rage$power >= 0.80)[1]]
power_at_200_rage <- round(s2_power_rage$power[s2_power_rage$sample_size == 210], 2) * 100
```

Our simulation-based power analysis sampled fractions of our 300-participant synthetic dataset 1,000 times per step, fitting both panel regressions with participant-clustered standard errors at each increment. For our primary outcome, power didn't cross the 80% conventional threshold until `r power_80_time` total participants (`r round(power_80_time/3)` per group). Our secondary frustration outcome tells a different story; the rage click model hits 80% power at just `r power_80_rage` participants (`r round(power_80_rage/3)` per group), meaning the frustration signal is strong enough to detect with far less data. We size the study to the harder-to-detect outcome to make sure we're adequately powered across both. Since the effect sizes were larger than scenario 1, we can see that we are able to achive sufficient power with much less samples.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.show: show
s2_power_time$outcome  <- "Total Time Spent"
s2_power_rage$outcome  <- "Rage Clicks"
power_combined <- rbind(s2_power_time, s2_power_rage)

# Plot
ggplot(power_combined, aes(x = sample_size, y = power, color = outcome)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = 0.82, label = "80% Power Threshold", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = "gray40") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(30, 300, by = 30)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Scenario 2 Power Analysis by Sample Size",
    x        = "Number of Participants",
    y        = "Statistical Power",
    color    = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Scenario 3: The High-Variance Persistence Model

To stress-test the boundary limits of our participant-clustered panel regression framework under realistic field noise, Scenario 3 inflates the population standard deviation to a conservative threshold ($\sigma = 135$ seconds) to capture a wider spectrum of natural cognitive processing speeds and individual fatigue thresholds. Our behavioral parameters within this high-variance deployment are grounded in the two previously mentioned papers. First, following the fMRI findings of [@baumeister1998ego], upfront warnings of high task difficulty trigger top-down mental preparation (phasic alertness) characterized by a graded suppression of task-irrelevant brain networks; we model this as a conscious, strategic slowing down that structurally extends the Hard/Accurate group's baseline solve times ($+40$ seconds). Second, when an objectively complex task is misleadingly framed as "Easy," it introduces an acute expectation violation. According to the effort-decoupling frameworks identified by [@ayolov2025rage], a heightened expectancy of success selectively drives individuals to engage in prolonged, stubborn verification processes to protect their self-perceived cognitive capability. We capture this ego-protective track by dropping the Easy/Understated group's 5-minute skip prompt quit probability to just $15\%$, significantly extending their time-to-quit relative to the accurate group ($35\%$) and control ($55\%$).

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: hide
# Generating data for Scenario 3: High Variance & Persistence
scenario_3_d <- generate_scenario_data(
  N = 300,        # Total sample size
  n_rounds = 5,   # 5 rounds of gameplay
  
  # Solve Time Baseline & Shifting Effects
  mean_solve_time   = 270,   
  sd_solve_time     = 135,   # Inflated variance to simulate noisy real-world data
  hard_solve_effect = 40,    # Accurate label takes noticeably longer (+40s)
  easy_solve_effect = 20,    # Understated label takes slightly longer (+20s)
  
  # 5-Minute Prompt Quit Probabilities (Lower prob = longer persistence)
  p_quit_5_control = 0.55,   # Baseline: 55% quit at 5 mins
  p_quit_5_hard    = 0.35,   # Accurate: 35% quit
  p_quit_5_easy    = 0.15,   # Understated: Only 15% quit (Stubborn persistence)
  
  # Frustration Metrics (Poisson expected average events)
  mean_rage_control = 0.4,   
  mean_rage_hard    = 0.7,   
  mean_rage_easy    = 1.1    
)

# Reshape and relevel for regression
scenario_3_long <- convert_to_long_format(scenario_3_d)
scenario_3_long[, treatment := relevel(factor(treatment), ref = "no_label")]

# Run regressions for Scenario 3
s3 <- lm(total_time_spent ~ treatment * round_num, data = scenario_3_long)
frustration_s3 <- lm(rage_clicks ~ treatment * round_num, data = scenario_3_long)

# Compute clustered standard errors (by id)
s3_clustered_se <- sqrt(diag(vcovCL(s3, cluster = ~id, data = scenario_3_long)))
frustration_clustered_se <- sqrt(diag(vcovCL(frustration_s3, cluster = ~id, data = scenario_3_long)))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| warning: false
#| message: false
stargazer(
  s3, frustration_s3,
  type = "latex",
  title = "Scenario 3: Panel Regression with High Group Variance",
  style = "default",
  se = list(s3_clustered_se, frustration_clustered_se),
  covariate.labels = c(
    "Easy / Understated Label",
    "Hard / Accurate Label",
    "Round Number",
    "Interaction: Easy Label x Round",
    "Interaction: Hard Label x Round"
  ),
  dep.var.labels = c("Total Seconds Spent", "Rage Clicks"),
  notes = c("Standard errors are clustered at the unique participant (id) level."),
  notes.append = FALSE,
  digits = 2
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# Model 1 (Time Spent) Variables
s3_intercept        <- round(coef(s3)["(Intercept)"], 1)
s3_easy_time_coef   <- round(coef(s3)["treatmenteasy_label"], 1)
s3_hard_time_coef   <- round(coef(s3)["treatmenthard_label"], 1)
s3_round_time_coef  <- round(coef(s3)["round_num"], 1)
s3_easy_time_inter  <- round(coef(s3)["treatmenteasy_label:round_num"], 1)
s3_hard_time_inter  <- round(coef(s3)["treatmenthard_label:round_num"], 1)

# Model 2 (Rage Clicks) Variables
s3_rage_intercept   <- round(coef(frustration_s3)["(Intercept)"], 2)
s3_easy_rage_coef   <- round(coef(frustration_s3)["treatmenteasy_label"], 2)
s3_hard_rage_coef   <- round(coef(frustration_s3)["treatmenthard_label"], 2)
s3_easy_rage_inter  <- round(coef(frustration_s3)["treatmenteasy_label:round_num"], 2)
```

The panel regression model successfully isolated the structural behavioral tracks across all 1,500 observation points (300 participants observed over 5 distinct rounds) despite high individual variance. For Model 1 (Total Seconds Spent), the baseline group intercept settled at `r round(coef(s3)["(Intercept)"], 2)` seconds, with both the Easy/Understated label group (+`r round(coef(s3)["treatmenteasy_label"], 2)`s, $p < 0.01$) and the Hard/Accurate label group (+`r round(coef(s3)["treatmenthard_label"], 2)`s, $p < 0.01$) spending significantly more time per round than the control group. The main effect of the Round Number trend (`r round(coef(s3)["round_num"], 2)`s) and both round-level interaction terms (Easy × Round: `r round(coef(s3)["treatmenteasy_label:round_num"], 2)`s; Hard × Round: `r round(coef(s3)["treatmenthard_label:round_num"], 2)`s) failed to reach statistical significance, verifying that these distinct behavioral tracks remain stable across all gameplay rounds. For Model 2 (Rage Clicks), the baseline control group averaged a minimal `r round(coef(frustration_s3)["(Intercept)"], 2)` clicks per round; however, the frustration signal was cleanly detected, showing an additional `r round(coef(frustration_s3)["treatmenteasy_label"], 2)` clicks per round in the understated condition ($p < 0.01$) and a far more muted, though still significant, increase of `r round(coef(frustration_s3)["treatmenthard_label"], 2)` clicks in the accurate condition ($p < 0.05$). Interestingly, a statistically significant negative interaction effect emerged for the understated group across rounds (Easy × Round: `r round(coef(frustration_s3)["treatmenteasy_label:round_num"], 2)`, $p < 0.05$), capturing a clear behavioral cooling-off or exhaustion trend where frustration spikes begin to attenuate as participants progress through the session.

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Run power loops for primary outcome (Total Time Spent)
s3_power_time <- run_power_analysis(
  long_dataset = scenario_3_long,
  outcome_var  = "total_time_spent",
  target_coef  = "treatmenteasy_label",
  power_loops  = 1000
)

# Run power loops for secondary outcome (Rage Clicks)
s3_power_rage <- run_power_analysis(
  long_dataset = scenario_3_long,
  outcome_var  = "rage_clicks",
  target_coef  = "treatmenteasy_label",
  power_loops  = 1000
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig.show: show
#| fig.width: 8
#| fig.height: 5.5

# Standardize outcome flags for the Scenario 3 frame
s3_power_time$outcome  <- "Total Time Spent"
s3_power_rage$outcome  <- "Rage Clicks"
s3_power_combined      <- rbind(s3_power_time, s3_power_rage)

# Identify exact empirical 80% threshold crossing points for the text write-up
s3_power_80_time <- s3_power_time$sample_size[which(s3_power_time$power >= 0.80)[1]]
s3_power_80_rage <- s3_power_rage$sample_size[which(s3_power_rage$power >= 0.80)[1]]

# Independent Scenario 3 Plot Layout
ggplot(s3_power_combined, aes(x = sample_size, y = power, color = outcome)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = 0.82, label = "80% Power Threshold", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = "gray40") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(30, 300, by = 30)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Scenario 3 Power Analysis by Sample Size",
    x        = "Number of Participants",
    y        = "Statistical Power",
    color    = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
s3_time_target <- ifelse(is.na(s3_power_80_time), "more than 300", as.character(s3_power_80_time))
s3_time_group  <- ifelse(is.na(s3_power_80_time), "100", as.character(round(s3_power_80_time / 3)))
s3_rage_target <- ifelse(is.na(s3_power_80_rage), "more than 300", as.character(s3_power_80_rage))
s3_rage_group  <- ifelse(is.na(s3_power_80_rage), "100", as.character(round(s3_power_80_rage / 3)))
```

Our simulation-based power analysis sampled fractions of our 300-participant synthetic dataset 1,000 times per step, fitting both panel regressions with participant-clustered standard errors at each increment to mirror our real data analysis plan.

For our primary outcome, power did not cross the conventional 80% threshold until reaching `r s3_time_target` total participants (`r s3_time_group` per group). This delayed power acquisition highlights the statistical consequence of the inflated population variance ($\sigma = 135$ seconds) modeled in this scenario. Because individual behavioral noise spans a wider spectrum, it is noticeably more difficult for the panel regression to cleanly detach the true framing treatment effect from natural background variance.

On the other hand, our secondary frustration outcome tells a different story. Despite the increased variance background, the localized behavior of the rage clicks is strong enough that the model achieves 80% power at just `r s3_rage_target` participants (`r s3_rage_group` per group). This confirms that the distinct frustration signal driven by cognitive dissonance is statistically robust and highly detectable even within a deeply noisy deployment environment.

# References

Lu H, Linden DV, Bakker AB. Disentangling the effects of task difficulty and effort on flow experience. Psychol Res. 2025 Jun 26;89(4):113. doi: 10.1007/s00426-025-02128-x. PMID: 40569444; PMCID: PMC12202627.

de Dreu MJ, Schouwenaars IT, Rutten GM, Ramsey NF, Jansma JM. Brain Activity Associated With Expected Task Difficulty. Front Hum Neurosci. 2019 Aug 28;13:286. doi: 10.3389/fnhum.2019.00286. PMID: 31555110; PMCID: PMC6724610.
